perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE 7 OWNERSHIP AND RETURN OF MATERIALS --------------------------------- Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall immediately surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information, it being distinctly understood that all such writings, physical forms of expression and other things are the exclusive property of Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE 7 OWNERSHIP AND RETURN OF MATERIALS --------------------------------- Covenantor shall immediately surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information, it being distinctly understood that all such writings, physical forms of expression and other things are the exclusive property of Buyer. Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer,
perturbation.explanation: The placement of the phrase 'Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer' has been moved to the end of the section, reducing the immediacy of the obligation to return materials. This weakens the clause and contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets upon termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying the statement regarding termination diminishes the clear obligation to return confidential information *immediately* upon termination, potentially hindering the protection of trade secrets as envisioned by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) which emphasizes preventing misappropriation. The changed text suggests that the return obligation is less immediate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intent of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as it weakens the immediacy of the obligation to return confidential information upon termination of employment. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this weakening. While the provided snippet only identifies the citation of the CUTSA as CIV 3426, the explanation suggests that delaying the return of materials contradicts the purpose of the act, which is to prevent misappropriation. By moving the termination clause to the end, the obligation is no longer explicitly tied to the moment of termination, creating a potential loophole where the employee could retain the information for some time after termination. This directly undermines the protection the original clause provided and intended to uphold.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE 11 ATTORNEYS' FEES --------------- In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover all of such party's reasonable attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all appeals or petitions relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE 11 ATTORNEYS' FEES --------------- In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover all of such party's reasonable attorneys' fees. expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all petitions relating thereto, unless otherwise agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless otherwise agreed upon" at the end of the attorney's fees clause introduces an ambiguity that could be interpreted as allowing a separate, perhaps unwritten, agreement to supersede the clause. This undermines the clarity and enforceability of the clause, as required by contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The phrase "unless otherwise agreed upon" introduces uncertainty into the attorneys' fees provision, potentially undermining its enforceability. Under California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021, parties can agree to attorney's fees, but the added phrase opens the door for disputes regarding whether an agreement existed and its terms, creating enforceability issues not present in the original clause. It also is unclear if this 'agreement' needs to be written.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The added phrase "unless otherwise agreed upon" creates a potential legal contradiction. The original contract clearly dictates that the prevailing party shall recover attorney's fees. The changed text introduces ambiguity, suggesting that this may not always be the case if another agreement exists. This is directly related to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021, which states that "the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties". While CCP 1021 permits agreements on attorney's fees, the added phrase weakens the primary agreement within the contract, opening the door to disputes about whether the separate agreement supersedes the contract's attorney's fees clause. Therefore, it creates an ambiguity that undermines the initial clause, fitting the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.
